title: The Air Quality and Well-Being Effects of Low Emission Zones
notes: EVENT STUDY REVIEWED. Sarmiento, Wagner & Zaklan (2023) JEEM. Low emission zones in German cities reduce PM10 pollution. Staggered adoption 2008-2016. CS-DD is the paper's primary estimator (att_gt with DR, nevertreated). 588 stations, 14 years (2005-2018). Raw sample (stations inside/near LEZ). Clustering at municipality level. No covariates in CS spec (xformla=~1). Paper's TWFE uses daily data with weather controls; our TWFE is on yearly data without controls for comparability with CS-DD. EVENT STUDY (5 estimators): All converge — growing negative effect from h=0 (-0.3 to -0.9) to h=5 (-1.9 to -2.4). Pre-trends flat for TWFE/SA/Gardner; CS-NT/CS-NYT slightly noisy at far pre-periods. TWFE vs CS-NT gap (1.24 vs 1.95) is from aggte(dynamic) weighting late horizons equally. VCOV warning on SA (small cohorts 2010/2015/2016). No heterogeneity variables in data (no state/region/station_type). Script: event_study_228.R. | AUDIT NOTE (2026-04-19): TWFE WARN is DATA GRANULARITY not implementation — paper uses daily data + weather ctrls; our TWFE uses yearly aggregated data without ctrls for comparability with CS-DID (paper's headline estimator). 0 controls → no Spec A/B/C decomposition. CS-NT≈CS-NYT (0.3% gap) confirms never-treated pool is clean (LESSON 6 positive case: when NT and NYT agree, CS-NT is defensible). Paper-auditor verdict NOT_APPLICABLE is legitimate: paper does not report a static TWFE in any table; the narrative value -1.21 on p.10 is approximate and on the daily+weather spec. MODERATE rating reflects the granularity gap, not a bug.
